---
pagetitle: "Skill Usage and Design"
title: "[Skill Usage and Design]{.amber}"
title-slide-attributes:
  data-background-color: "#2C3E50"
  data-background-image: figs/banner.png
  data-background-size: 24%
  data-background-position: right 20% center
  class: dark-centered
  style: text-align:left;
---

## Recap & today {.light-centered .center}

In Part 1 we placed **skills** in the **Behaviors** layer of the four-component model.

<br>

Three sections today, ~25 minutes:

1. **[Common Skills]{.teal}** â€” what people actually use
2. **[Skill Design]{.amber}** â€” how to write your own
3. **[Skill Symlinks]{.coral}** â€” how to share them across projects

## [Section 1]{.amber}<br>Common Skills {.dark-centered .center}

## What a skill really is {.light-centered .center}

- A **directory** under `.claude/skills/`
- Contains a `SKILL.md` file
- Optionally: scripts, assets, data, reference files
- The agent discovers, explores, and uses all of it

<br>

::: {.centered-container style="width: 70%;"}
Skill = `SKILL.md` + (optional) supporting files
:::

## A minimal `SKILL.md` {.light-centered .center}

```markdown
.claude/skills/api-conventions/SKILL.md
---
name: api-conventions
description: REST API design conventions for our services
---
# API Conventions
- Use kebab-case for URL paths
- Use camelCase for JSON properties
- Always include pagination for list endpoints
- Version APIs in the URL path (/v1/, /v2/)
```

<br>

> Two ways skills run: **auto-trigger** on context, or **manual** via `/name`

## Where skills come from {.light-centered .center}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="33%"}
### [Plugins]{.teal}

- `everything-claude-code`
- `claude-plugins-official`
- Install via `/plugin`
:::

::: {.column width="34%"}
### [Curated sets]{.amber}

- tw93's [Waza](https://github.com/tw93/waza)
- 8 short skills
- `/think` `/design` `/hunt` `/check` `/write` `/learn` `/read` `/health`
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
### [Your own]{.coral}

- The most important source
- 60â€“80% of daily use after a few months
- We'll write one in Section 2
:::
:::

## What people use them for {.light-centered .center}

| Category | Example |
|---|---|
| **Code review** | `/review`, `/security-review` |
| **Refactor & cleanup** | `/simplify`, `/refactor-clean` |
| **Test discipline** | `/tdd`, `/test-coverage`, `/e2e` |
| **Build / verify** | `/build-fix`, `/verify`, `/quality-gate` |
| **Documentation** | `/update-docs`, `/init` |
| **PR & git** | `/commit`, `/checkpoint` |
| **Knowledge** | `/think`, `/learn`, `/read`, `/docs-lookup` |

<br>

> The 80/20 rule kicks in fast. Optimize the handful you actually use.

## Plugins vs standalone skills {.light-centered .center}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### [Standalone skill]{.teal}

- One `SKILL.md` in `.claude/skills/`
- Quick, self-contained
- Easy to write and share
- Where most people start
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### [Plugin]{.amber}

- Bundles multiple skills + MCPs + hooks
- For distribution to a team
- For coherent skill sets
- Graduate here once you have 3â€“4 related skills
:::
:::

## [Section 2]{.amber}<br>Skill Design {.dark-centered .center}

## Anatomy of `SKILL.md` {.light-centered .center}

Two parts: **frontmatter** (YAML) + **body** (markdown).

```markdown
---
name: fix-issue
description: Fix a GitHub issue
disable-model-invocation: true
---
Analyze and fix the GitHub issue: $ARGUMENTS.

1. Use `gh issue view` to get the issue details
2. Search the codebase for relevant files
3. Implement the fix
4. Write and run tests
5. Create a descriptive commit, push, and open a PR
```

<br>

- **`name`** â†’ becomes `/name`. Keep short.
- **`description`** â†’ one sentence. Used for auto-trigger.
- **`disable-model-invocation: true`** â†’ opt out of auto-trigger (for side-effect skills)

## The load-bearing section {.dark-centered .center}

> *"The most valuable content in any skill is the **Gotchas** section containing common mistakes based on real problems Claude encounters when using your skill."*
>
> â€” Anthropic, *Building Skills for Claude*

<br>

::: {.centered-container style="width: 75%;"}
Gotchas are where your tribal knowledge lives.
:::

## A Gotchas-rich skill {.light-centered .center}

```markdown
---
name: deploy-staging
description: Deploy current branch to staging
disable-model-invocation: true
---

## Steps
1. ...

## Gotchas
- The migration must complete BEFORE the API redeploys,
  or requests hit the new schema with the old code.
- `npm run build` succeeds even if env vars are missing.
  Verify with `npm run build:check` before deploying.
- The CDN cache takes 90 seconds to purge.
  Don't declare success until the smoke test passes.
```

<br>

> Difference between "kind of works" and **actually reliable**.

## Design principle 1 {.light-centered .center}

::: {.centered-container style="width: 75%; font-size: 1.4em;"}
[Less is more.]{.amber}
:::

<br>

- Fewer skills, shorter names â†’ easier recall â†’ actually used
- The everything-bundle wins **once**; the focused set wins **daily**
- Heuristics:
  - Long names = probably one skill too many
  - Aim for **4â€“8 character** slash commands
  - **6â€“10 active skills** is the sweet spot

## Design principle 2 {.light-centered .center}

::: {.centered-container style="width: 75%; font-size: 1.4em;"}
[Define the negatives.]{.coral}
:::

<br>

- LLMs default to broadly useful â†’ trigger too often
- Saying what a skill should **not** do sharpens targeting
- Compare:
  - âťŚ *"code review for security issues"*
  - âś… *"code review for security issues â€” do NOT run on docs, refactors, or formatting-only diffs"*

> Underused. Steal it.

## How to actually write a skill {.light-centered .center}

Don't start from a blank page.

1. **Do the task by hand once** with Claude Code. Get it right.
2. **Ask Claude to extract a `SKILL.md`** from the session â€” including a Gotchas section based on what went wrong.
3. **Drop it in** `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`. Test on the next similar task.
4. **Refine over time.** Misfires â†’ update Gotchas. Extra work â†’ add negative examples.

<br>

::: {.centered-container style="width: 80%;"}
Complete a full output first â†’ then ask Claude to extract a rule from it.
:::

## Skills vs `CLAUDE.md` vs rules {.light-centered .center}

| Use | When | Loaded |
|---|---|---|
| **`CLAUDE.md`** | Applies to *every* session | Always |
| **`rules/*.md`** | Topical knowledge (style, conventions) | On demand by Claude |
| **Skills** | Reusable workflows (steps to *do* something) | On invocation |

<br>

- *"Always behave this way"* â†’ `CLAUDE.md` (short)
- *"When you need to know about X"* â†’ `rules/X.md`
- *"When you need to do X"* â†’ `skills/X/SKILL.md`

## Skill anti-patterns {.light-centered .center}

- **Kitchen-sink skill** â€” a `/do-everything` that branches into ten workflows. Split it.
- **Duplicate-of-CLAUDE.md skill** â€” restates rules already loaded every session. Delete one.
- **Auto-trigger landmine** â€” side-effect skill (deploy, push, delete) without `disable-model-invocation: true`. The model will call it.
- **Unverified skill** â€” steps without a "how to confirm it worked" section. You'll catch failures by hand.

## [Section 3]{.amber}<br>Skill Symlinks {.dark-centered .center}

## The problem {.light-centered .center}

You write a great `/commit` skill in **Project A**.<br>Project B needs the same skill. Project C also.

<br>

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="33%"}
### Copy-paste

Drift the moment you improve one. âťŚ
:::

::: {.column width="34%"}
### Bundle as plugin

Real solution. Overkill for one or two skills. âš ď¸Ź
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
### Symlink

Lightweight middle path. âś…
:::
:::

## How symlinks work in Claude Code {.light-centered .center}

::: {.centered-container style="width: 80%;"}
Claude Code follows symlinks transparently.<br>It reads and edits the **target** file, not the link.
:::

<br>

- Keep one canonical copy of a skill anywhere on disk
- Link to it from any project's `.claude/skills/`
- Update once â†’ all linked projects see the change

## Two patterns {.light-centered .center}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Pattern A â€” global folder

- Canonical copy at `~/.claude/skills/<name>/`
- Each project: `ln -s` into `.claude/skills/`
- Add link to project `.gitignore`
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Pattern B â€” skills repo

- Canonical copy in `~/code/my-skills/`
- Each project links what it wants
- Bonus: skills repo is version-controlled and shareable
:::
:::

## A complete example {.light-centered .center}

```bash
# One-time: create the canonical skill
mkdir -p ~/.claude/skills/commit
cat > ~/.claude/skills/commit/SKILL.md <<'EOF'
---
name: commit
description: Stage relevant files and create a conventional commit
---
Stage relevant changes and create a commit message
following conventional-commits format. Do NOT amend.
EOF

# Per project: link it in
cd ~/code/my-project
mkdir -p .claude/skills
ln -s ~/.claude/skills/commit .claude/skills/commit

# Don't ship the link
echo ".claude/skills/commit" >> .gitignore
```

<br>

> Now `/commit` works here. Update the canonical once â†’ every linked project gets the change.

## Important guardrails {.light-centered .center}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### âś… Do

- Symlink **individual skill folders**
- Add symlinks to `.gitignore`
- Track the canonical skill in git
- Use absolute paths
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### âťŚ Don't

- Symlink the entire `.claude/`<br>(it has project-specific config)
- Commit the symlink to git<br>(points to your home dir)
- Symlink across machines without a sync strategy
:::
:::

## When to graduate to a plugin {.light-centered .center}

Symlinks are great for **1â€“5 personal skills**.

<br>

The signal it's time to move on:

- Coherent set of skills that belong together (e.g., a "git workflow" set)
- Want to share with a teammate or publish
- Want to bundle skills + MCP + hooks into one install

<br>

::: {.centered-container style="width: 70%;"}
Same source of truth. Plus distribution.
:::

## Cross-tool sharing {.light-centered .center}

If you also use Codex, OpenClaw, or other CLI agents:

- Each agent looks for skills in a **different directory**
- Tools like [skillshare](https://github.com/runkids/skillshare) maintain one skills directory and create symlinks into each agent's expected location
- Single source of truth across **multiple agents**

<br>

> Worth knowing about. Not required to start.

## Three takeaways {.light-centered .center}

1. **Common skills.** Most useful ones come from your own work. Plugins and curated sets are the seed. Optimize the **6â€“10** you use daily.

2. **Skill design.** `SKILL.md` = frontmatter + body. **Gotchas section is load-bearing.** Two principles: **less is more**, **define the negatives**. Extract from success, don't start blank.

3. **Skill symlinks.** One canonical source, `ln -s` into each project, `.gitignore` the link. Symlink individual skills, never the whole `.claude/`. Graduate to plugins when the set grows.

## Up next {.dark-centered .center}

### [Part 3:]{.amber} Harness Engineering

- Components of a **harness** (planner / generator / evaluator)
- Practices that make long-running agent work reliable
- A staged path to designing your own
